solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1571I - Physical Examination
Think of the function that can tell if it's possible to start at minute $$$x$$$ and visit all doctors by the rules in the statement. This is solvable in $$$O(n \log n)$$$. That's a very common greedy, where you basically take the doctor who closes the earliest among the open ones and put him in the next minute.
If there exists a way to visit everyone, this solution will find it (and vice verse, the proof is left as an exercise for the reader).
Otherwise, there are two options for when it can fail. First, there might be no doctors to visit at some minute. Second, there might be two or more doctors that close at the same minute and weren't visited prior to it.
It's claimed that the function that returns $$$-1$$$ for the fail of the first type, $$$0$$$ for a valid start and $$$1$$$ for the fail of the second type, is monotonous.
Thus, we can perform a binary search on it to obtain a solution in $$$O(n \log n \log \mathit{MAX})$$$.
The idea of the proof is the following.
Consider a fail of the first type. What happens if you move $$$x$$$ one to the left? There was a prefix of doctors who got visited at $$$x$$$, $$$x+1$$$ and so on. Then there was a minute with no doctors. Since that minute is free, no doctor other than from this prefix is open. If there are $$$y$$$ doctors in it, then they have to fill up $$$y+1$$$ minutes, which is impossible. Adding a minute to the left and possibly removing one from the right still leave it at least $$$y+1$$$. Thus, it's still a fail of the first type.
Now consider a fail of the second type. What happens if you move $$$x$$$ one to the right? Minute $$$x+n$$$ is added and minute $$$x$$$ is removed. However, even if minute $$$x$$$ wasn't removed, an extra minute on the right doesn't help in any way. The minute on the left can only cause more collisions between doctors. Thus, it's still a fail of the second type.
Beware that some $$$x$$$ might be a fail of both types if the answer doesn't exist.

solutions.title: Solution (awoo)

())

split

toInt
()

=

()!!.
split

map
{
it

toInt
()

val ord
=

(

,

sortWith

:

[

-

[

=

:

fl

val cur
=

<

>(

[
it
]
})
var
j
=
0
var
res
=
0
for
(
t
in
m
until
m
+
n
)
{
while
(
j
<
n
&&
L
[
ord
[
j
]]
<=
t
)
{
cur
.
add
(

[
j
])
++
j
;

if
(

.
isEmpty
())

=
1

(
R
[

.
peek
()]
<
t

=
-
1

}
if
(
fl
)
ans
[
t
-
m
]
=
cur
.
peek
()
+
1
cur
.
poll
()
}
return
res
;
}
var
l
=
1
var
r
=
1e9
.
toInt
()
var
res
=
-
1

(
l
<=
r
)
{
val m
=
(
l
+
r
)
/
2
val tmp
=

(
m
,
false
)
if
(
tmp
==
0
)

res
=
m
break
}
if
(
tmp
==
-
1
)
{
r
=
m
-
1
}
else
{

=
m
+
1
}
}
println
(

)
if
(

!=
-
1
)

(

,

.
